structured_choices.data_source: Retrospective cohort of adult inpatient discharges from an integrated delivery network over 3 years (36 months). Data includes encounter-level EHR records (demographics, diagnoses [ICD-10], procedures [CPT], lab results with timestamps, vital signs, discharge disposition) and outpatient pharmacy claims (medication fills with dates and drug codes) for 24 months post-discharge. Index event: first discharge of an adult patient (age ≥18) from a general medicine service; follow-up window: 30 days post-discharge. Data quality: missing lab values and vitals are flagged; medication fill data is right-censored at health system boundary (external fills not captured). Deployment target distribution: similar inpatient population at the same health system, with the same data availability and missingness patterns.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: unplanned hospital readmission within 30 days of discharge (yes/no). Unplanned readmissions are identified by excluding scheduled/planned readmissions (flagged via admission type codes and presence of pre-admission scheduling indicators). Label is assigned at discharge and is fully observed by day 31 post-discharge; no censoring.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal (chronological) split to prevent leakage of future practice patterns, seasonal confounds, and medication fill trends. Cohort is sorted by discharge date. Training set: all discharges during months 1–24 (70% of time window). Validation set: discharges during months 25–30 (15% of time window). Test set: discharges during months 31–36 (15% of time window). Justification: Longitudinal EHR sequences and medication history exhibit strong autocorrelation; a random split would allow models to learn patterns from future data (e.g., hospital admission patterns in month 35 influencing training on month 5 discharges). A chronological split ensures that all temporal patterns in the training set precede those in validation and test, mimicking deployment where predictions are made at discharge with only historical information available. Time gaps between splits (1-month burn-in between train and validation, 1-month buffer before test) reduce contamination from overlapping patient cohorts and allow pharmacy claims to settle.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation strategies) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–24) using only data available at or before each patient's discharge date. Fitted transformers are then applied identically to validation and test sets without refitting. Justification: refitting on validation/test data would introduce indirect leakage of label information and future missingness patterns. Imputation models (e.g., KNN or mean) are fit on training data only to avoid biasing toward validation/test population characteristics.
structured_choices.features: Input feature groups: (1) Longitudinal EHR sequence: all encounters for each patient in the 24 months prior to index discharge, including visit type, primary/secondary diagnoses, procedures, lab result names and values with timestamps, vital signs (BP, HR, O2, temp) with timestamps, and medication orders. Encoded as timestamped event sequences. (2) Pre-discharge structured features (baseline): demographics (age, sex, insurance type), Charlson comorbidity index computed from all diagnoses in prior 24 months, hospital admission count in prior 12 months, ED visit count in prior 12 months, length of stay for index admission, discharge disposition (home, SNF, rehab), primary diagnosis category. (3) Medication fill history: all outpatient fills in the 24 months prior to discharge, including drug class, fill date, days supply. Encoded as a sequence. (4) Constructed features: time since last hospital admission, time since last ED visit, medication count in prior 90 days, count of high-risk medication classes (e.g., anticoagulants, opioids). Feature engineering does not introduce future information (e.g., post-discharge fills or readmission dates are held out).
structured_choices.model: Transformer-based sequence model: dual-encoder architecture with separate transformer encoders for the EHR event sequence and medication fill sequence, concatenated before a classification head. EHR encoder processes variable-length timestamped events (visits, labs, vitals) using learnable event embeddings and absolute positional encodings. Medication encoder processes fill events similarly. Both encoders use multi-head self-attention (8 heads, 2 layers, 256 hidden dimensions) with residual connections and layer normalization. Concatenated representations are passed through a 2-layer MLP (256 → 128 → 1 hidden units) with ReLU and dropout (0.2) for classification. Model is trained end-to-end with binary cross-entropy loss.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on the pre-discharge structured features only (demographics, Charlson index, admission/ED visit counts in prior 12 months, LOS, discharge disposition, primary diagnosis category). This baseline receives the same tuning effort as the transformer: hyperparameter search (L2 regularization strength via grid search on validation set), class-weight balancing to account for imbalance, and identical train/val/test splits. The baseline uses no longitudinal sequences or pharmacy data, making it a fair comparison of the architectural difference (structured features only vs. structured + sequences + pharmacy history). Both models are evaluated on the same test set under the same conditions.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC). Justification: The deployment context is constrained outreach: care managers have capacity to reach ~20–30% of discharged patients. AUPRC is the appropriate metric because it measures the model's ability to identify true readmissions (recall) while keeping false alarms low (precision) across decision thresholds, directly supporting threshold selection for prioritizing outreach. AUPRC is robust to class imbalance (unlike accuracy) and does not require choosing a single operating point a priori. The metric is computed on the held-out test set only, using predicted probabilities calibrated on the validation set.
secondary_metrics: Sensitivity and specificity at clinically relevant thresholds (e.g., threshold chosen to reach 25% of cohort)
Calibration slope and intercept (whether predicted probabilities match observed readmission rates in bins on validation set)
Net benefit (decision curve analysis) comparing the transformer to baseline and to a 'treat all' strategy
Performance stratified by data completeness (cohorts with complete vs. missing pharmacy data) to assess handling of differential missingness
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameters for both transformer and baseline are tuned via grid search over the validation set (months 25–30), selecting the model that maximizes AUPRC on validation predictions. The validation set is used once for this tuning; the test set is held out and touched only once at the end of the experiment to compute final metrics.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 31–36, ~15% of discharges) is reserved and not inspected until model selection is complete. After both models are finalized (trained on train set with hyperparameters selected via validation set), predictions are generated on the test set exactly once. All reported metrics (AUPRC, AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, calibration, decision curves) are computed on test set predictions only. No retraining or threshold adjustment is performed on test data.
structured_choices.confound_controls: 1. Temporal structure: chronological split prevents leakage of future admission trends, seasonality, and evolving clinical practices. 2. Medication fill differential missingness: The experiment stratifies test results by data completeness (pharmacy data available vs. unavailable/censored) to show whether the transformer's benefit holds in the complete-data subgroup and whether missingness handling is the driver. If the transformer's advantage is entirely in the missing-data group, this signals that the benefit may be due to implicit imputation rather than sequence learning. 3. Richer history vs. architectural benefit: The baseline receives all available structured features from the 24-month lookback (not just latest visit), controlling for the hypothesis that the transformer's benefit is simply from access to more history. The transformer's marginal gain over this history-aware baseline isolates the value of sequence modeling. 4. Held-out test set: The test cohort is temporally separated and never used for tuning, preventing optimistic bias and overfitting to test distribution. 5. Class imbalance: Both models use class weighting and are evaluated on AUPRC and AUROC (metrics robust to imbalance), not accuracy. 6. Reproducibility: random seeds are fixed; data preprocessing and model training code is version-controlled; the experiment is registered prior to running to prevent p-hacking. Scope: The experiment will establish whether the transformer model discriminates readmission better than structured logistic regression in this health system's population during the test period. It will not establish generalization to other health systems, other patient populations, or prospective deployment. The experiment does not establish whether the model reduces readmission rates in practice (only whether it predicts them); that is a separate RCT or quasi-experimental question.

design_narrative:
# Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Logistic Regression for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

## 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses a retrospective cohort of adult inpatient discharges from a single integrated delivery network over 36 months. The index event is discharge from a general medicine service; the outcome is any unplanned hospital readmission within 30 days, fully observed by day 31. The dataset includes encounter-level EHR data (demographics, diagnoses, procedures, labs, vitals) spanning 24 months prior to each discharge, plus outpatient pharmacy fill records for the same lookback window.

Known data quality issues are addressed as follows: missing lab values and vital signs are flagged during preprocessing and handled via imputation (fit on training data only); medication fills are right-censored at the health system boundary, meaning fills at outside pharmacies are unobserved. This missingness is deliberate and realistic; the experiment stratifies results by pharmacy data completeness to ensure the model's benefit does not rest entirely on implicit handling of missing data.

## 2. Split Strategy

A **temporal (chronological) split** is used to prevent leakage and preserve the causal structure of the data. Discharges are ordered by date and divided as follows:
- **Training set (months 1–24):** 70% of the time window; all discharges and their associated histories
- **Validation set (months 25–30):** 15% of the time window; used for hyperparameter selection
- **Test set (months 31–36):** 15% of the time window; held out until final evaluation

Justification: Longitudinal EHR sequences exhibit strong temporal autocorrelation and seasonality. A random split would allow the model to learn patterns from month 35 when training on month 5 discharges, introducing leakage. Conversely, a chronological split ensures that all temporal patterns in the training set temporally precede those in validation and test, mimicking the deployment scenario where predictions are made at discharge using only historical information. A 1-month burn-in buffer between train and validation, and another between validation and test, reduces contamination from overlapping patient cohorts and allows pharmacy claims to settle.

## 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, encoding) are **fit on the training set only** using information available at or before each patient's discharge date. They are then applied identically to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents indirect leakage of label information and future missingness patterns into validation and test.

Features are organized in three groups:
1. **Longitudinal EHR sequence:** All encounters in the 24 months prior to discharge (visits, labs, procedures, vitals), encoded as timestamped event sequences with learned embeddings and absolute positional encodings.
2. **Medication fill history:** All outpatient fills in the prior 24 months, encoded as a timestamped sequence.
3. **Structured pre-discharge features:** Demographics (age, sex, insurance), Charlson comorbidity index, admission and ED visit counts (prior 12 months), length of stay, discharge disposition, primary diagnosis category, and constructed features (time since last admission, medication count in prior 90 days, high-risk medication class indicators).

No feature uses post-discharge information (e.g., post-discharge fills or readmission dates are held out by design).

## 4. Models and Baseline

**Transformer Model:** A dual-encoder architecture with separate transformer encoders for the EHR sequence and medication sequence. Each encoder uses multi-head self-attention (8 heads, 2 transformer layers, 256 hidden dimensions) with residual connections and layer normalization. The concatenated representations pass through a 2-layer MLP (256 → 128 → 1) with ReLU, dropout (0.2), and binary cross-entropy loss. The model is trained end-to-end.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on structured features only (demographics, Charlson index, prior admission/ED counts, LOS, discharge disposition, primary diagnosis). The baseline receives **equivalent tuning effort**: hyperparameter search for L2 regularization strength via grid search on the validation set, class-weight balancing, and identical train/val/test splits. This ensures the comparison isolates the value of sequence modeling, not tuning disparity.

## 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC). This is the correct metric for the deployment context: care managers have limited outreach capacity (~20–30% of cohort). AUPRC measures how well the model identifies true readmissions (recall) while keeping false alarms low (precision) across decision thresholds, enabling data-driven threshold selection. AUPRC is robust to class imbalance and does not require choosing a single operating point a priori.

**Secondary metrics:**
- AUROC (discrimination across thresholds)
- Sensitivity and specificity at clinically relevant thresholds (e.g., threshold targeting 25% outreach)
- Calibration slope and intercept (validation set)
- Decision curve analysis (net benefit vs. baseline and treat-all strategies)
- Performance stratified by pharmacy data completeness (to validate that the transformer's benefit is not driven solely by implicit imputation of missing fills)

## 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both transformer and baseline are tuned via grid search on the validation set (months 25–30), selecting the model that maximizes AUPRC on validation predictions. The validation set is used once for this purpose. After tuning is complete, the test set (months 31–36) is held out and touched only once: all reported metrics are computed on test set predictions with no further retraining or threshold adjustment.

## 7. Controls for Confounds

**Temporal leakage:** Prevented by chronological split; future admission trends and seasonality cannot influence training data.

**Differential missingness in pharmacy data:** The experiment stratifies test results by whether pharmacy data is available vs. missing/censored. If the transformer's advantage is entirely in the missing-data group, this indicates that the benefit may be due to implicit handling of missingness rather than sequence modeling, qualifying the claim.

**History vs. architecture:** The baseline receives all available structured features from the 24-month lookback window, not just the latest visit. The transformer's marginal gain over this history-aware baseline isolates the value of sequence encoding.

**Reproducibility and bias prevention:** Random seeds are fixed; all code is version-controlled; the experiment design is registered prior to execution to prevent p-hacking.

**Scope:** The experiment establishes predictive discrimination in this health system's population during the test period. It does not establish generalization to other systems, other populations, or prospective clinical deployment. Impact on actual readmission rates is a separate RCT question.
